comments:
Summary:
This paper studies the effect of applying dropout to the inputs of max-pooling layers in convolutional neural networks. The authors show that max-pooling with dropout at training time is equivalent to sampling a pooled activation according to a multinomial distribution (with parameters determined by the retaining probability p). They propose probabilistic weighted pooling at test time as a principled model-averaging approximation, evaluate its performance against standard max-pooling and scaled max-pooling, and empirically explore dropout applied to convolutional and fully-connected layers. Experiments on MNIST, CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 show that probabilistic weighted pooling outperforms alternatives and that using max-pooling dropout together with fully-connected dropout yields strong results (state-of-the-art on MNIST among non-augmented methods, competitive results on CIFAR datasets). The paper also compares max-pooling dropout to stochastic pooling.

Strengths:
- Clear, intuitive theoretical insight: showing training-time max-pooling dropout corresponds to multinomial sampling is a useful perspective that unifies and clarifies connections to stochastic pooling.
- A simple, principled test-time procedure (probabilistic weighted pooling) follows naturally and is computationally cheap.
- Empirical evaluation across three standard datasets (MNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100) and multiple architectures supports the claims. The comparison with stochastic pooling is informative.
- Practical recommendations (e.g. combining max-pooling and fully-connected dropout; sensitivity of performance to retaining probability p) are valuable for practitioners.

Weaknesses and issues to address / clarify:
- Scope of experiments: all presented experiments use relatively small/medium CNN architectures and do not include large-scale image recognition (e.g. ImageNet) or experiments with data augmentation. It is therefore unclear how well the findings scale to modern, very deep/wide networks or interact with standard augmentation practices.
- Retaining-probability sensitivity: results show a U-shaped dependence on p; this hyperparameter sensitivity is important but not fully explored. More guidance on selecting p depending on architecture or pooling region size would strengthen the work.
- Simultaneous dropout interactions: the observation that applying convolutional and pooling dropout together can hurt performance is interesting; however the paper lacks an analysis or hypothesis explaining when/why this happens.
- The theoretical presentation, while intuitive, could be tightened: the treatment of the "all units dropped" event (the a_0^(0) term) and practical handling when this occurs could be stated more explicitly. Formal bounds or further justification of probabilistic weighted pooling as an approximation to the ensemble average (beyond intuitive argument and empirical evidence) would add rigor.
- Comparisons to other regularizers: while stochastic pooling is compared, broader comparisons (e.g. DropConnect, maxout with/without dropout) are only discussed qualitatively in places; direct empirical comparisons would help situate the contribution.
- Manuscript quality: the provided text contains several duplicated sentences, minor typographical and formatting issues that should be cleaned up for clarity.

Suggestions for improvement / further experiments:
- Evaluate on larger/deeper modern CNNs and, where feasible, an ImageNet-scale experiment or at least larger-scale benchmarks, and include standard data augmentation to show whether the improvements persist.
- More systematic ablations: effect of pooling region size, effect of activation sparsity on the multinomial probabilities, and more extensive sweep over retaining probabilities with recommendations.
- Analyze the cause of degraded performance when combining certain dropout placements (e.g. convolutional + pooling), perhaps via diagnostic metrics (activation sparsity, gradient norms, training dynamics) or visualization.
- Compare with a broader set of baselines (DropConnect, maxout networks, and other pooling strategies) in matched settings.
- Improve presentation and notation (clarify the handling of the all-zero pooled case, remove repeated paragraphs, fix typesetting errors).

Overall assessment:
This is a well-motivated, clearly explained contribution that provides a useful perspective on dropout in pooling layers and a practical test-time pooling rule. The empirical results support the claims on the evaluated benchmarks. The paper would be stronger with larger-scale experiments, deeper analysis of hyperparameter sensitivity and interactions among dropout placements, and a cleaner manuscript. Nevertheless, the contribution is of clear interest to the deep learning community and likely to be adopted in practice.
